summary: Cathy O'Neil examines New York's investigation into banks using opaque databases to deny people banking services, effectively banishing over a million lower-income Americans from the financial system. She counters libertarian free-market arguments by noting banks are taxpayer-supported and should serve the public. More fundamentally, she argues we focus too much on defending against bad things happening while ignoring the denial of opportunities—good things not happening—which compounds into systematic harm for vulnerable populations.
key_points: Opaque, error-prone databases are being used to disqualify people from basic banking, with the 'underbanked' population growing 10% since 2009
Banks on taxpayer support have a reasonable obligation to provide services to most people as part of their public responsibility
Constitutional rights focus on preventing bad things, but lack protection for access to basic opportunities like banking, education, and fair mortgages
The cumulative effect of denied opportunities narrows future options just as surely as active harm would
Free market corrections for database errors don't adequately address the systematic exclusion of vulnerable populations
money_quotes: Negative reports stemming from the databases have effectively banished more than a million lower-income Americans from the financial system.
We care about and defend ourselves from our constitutional rights being taken away but we have much less energy to defend ourselves against good things not happening to us.
It's not written into the constitution that we all deserve a good checking account, nor a good college education, nor good terms on a mortgage, and so on.
Such things are basic ingredients for a comfortable existence. Yet these services are rare if not nonexistent for a huge and swelling part of our society.
Instead of only worrying about the truly bad things that might happen to our vulnerable citizens, I personally spend just as much time worrying about the good things that might not happen.
Lots of good things not happening add up to bad things happening: they all narrow future options.
key_insight: The denial of basic opportunities through opaque algorithmic systems is just as harmful as active discrimination—good things not happening to vulnerable people systematically narrows their futures.
